John writes to people who claim to know Jesus, and he will not let them rest in comfortable assertion. Knowing Him is not a private conviction; it shows itself in obedience to His commandments. There is no hidden faith, no secret discipleship that divorces belief from behavior. When someone says they know Christ but refuses to keep His word, John is blunt: that person is a liar. The truth is not in them. This is not judgment born of harshness but of clarity. Love itself demands this honesty. If we truly love someone, we do not pretend that their rebellion against Him is compatible with knowing Him. Yet notice the grace woven through this severity: if we do sin—when we do sin—we have an Advocate. Jesus Christ stands before the Father as our righteous One, the atoning sacrifice not just for our sins but for the sins of the whole world. We are not orphaned in our failure. We are defended by the One who paid the price for our guilt.

The test of genuine faith becomes unmistakably clear in how we treat one another. Hatred of a brother or sister, no matter what words are spoken about light or love or faith, is a return to darkness. The person who hates is blinded and does not know where he is going. This is devastating language. It means that hatred is not merely a sin among sins; it is a sign of spiritual blindness, a loss of orientation toward God Himself. Conversely, love of the brother keeps us in the light, and there is no cause of stumbling. The ancient commandment and the new commandment are one: love. In the old law it was required. In the new covenant, Christ has embodied it perfectly, and now His life in us makes it possible. The darkness is fading and the true light is already shining. We live in that overlap of ages, where the old creation is dying and the new has already been born in Christ.

John turns his attention to a specific threat: those who claim to be Christians but deny that Jesus is the Christ, the Messiah, the Son of God. These antichrists have gone out from the community, and their departure has exposed what was always true—they were never truly of the faith. But for those who remain, there is assurance. You have an anointing from the Holy One. You know the truth. You have not been left without defense or discernment. What was taught to you from the beginning—that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God—that is what you must hold fast. It is the promise given to you: eternal life. This is not a reward for the elite or the especially spiritual. It is the gift promised to all who confess the Son and remain in the truth they have heard.

The reflection ends where all of John's thought leads: to Christ's appearing. Remain in Him, so that when He appears, you may be confident and unashamed. This is the hope that anchors everything—not confidence in your own consistency or strength, but confidence in His return and His acceptance of you through the righteousness of Christ. If you know that He is righteous, you also know that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of Him. You are His children, born of Him, carrying His character. The life you live in obedience, in love, in resistance to the world's desires, is not your own achievement; it is the sign that you belong to Him and that He is at work within you. Stand firm. The way grows shorter, and His coming draws near.
